file_name: Pukhta and Rout - 2024 - Hybrid optimization assisted deep ensemble classification framework for skin cancer detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a hybrid optimization assisted deep ensemble classification framework for skin cancer detection. It utilizes an ensemble classifier combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) models. The weights of these deep learning models are tuned using the Hawks Updated Blue Monkey Algorithm with Gaussian Map Randomness (HUBM-GMR) optimization technique. The task involves segmentation using Improved DBSCAN and feature extraction (GLCM, LBP, LVP, CSLTP) for classifying skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a 'Hybrid optimization assisted deep ensemble classification framework for skin cancer detection.' It details a specific methodology involving pre-processing, segmentation using Improved DBSCAN, feature extraction (GLCM, LBP, LVP, CSLTP), and classification using an ensemble of CNN, LSTM, and Bi-GRU models optimized by the HUBM-GMR algorithm. The paper includes a 'Simulation setup' and 'Results' section where the proposed model is validated against other classifiers using datasets from Kaggle, reporting performance metrics such as Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Specificity, Precision, and MCC. While it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is to introduce and validate a new technical solution, not to summarize prior literature.

title: Hybrid optimization assisted deep ensemble classification framework for skin cancer detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid optimization-assisted deep ensemble classification framework for automated skin cancer detection to address challenges in early diagnosis. The methodology employs a median filter for pre-processing, followed by lesion segmentation using an improved DBSCAN algorithm. Key texture features, including GLCM, LBP, LVP, and improved CSLTP, are extracted from the segmented regions. Classification is performed using an ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU) models. The weights of these deep learning models are optimized using the Hawks Updated Blue Monkey Algorithm with Gaussian Map Randomness (HUBM-GMR). The framework was validated on two datasets: the ISIC dataset and the HAM10000 dataset, demonstrating improved performance over existing methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant in the ISIC dataset (Dataset 1) and HAM10000 dataset (Dataset 2). The paper notes 288,000 occurrences of malignant skin cancer (MM) worldwide in 2018.
Classified as benign in the ISIC dataset (Dataset 1) and HAM10000 dataset (Dataset 2). The paper mentions distinguishing between a birthmark and melanoma as a challenge.

Mentioned in the abstract (1 million occurrences in 2018) and introduction as a category of skin cancer originating from basal or squamous cells, but the experimental classification tasks focus on malignant vs. benign moles (melanoma vs. nevus).

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a deep ensemble classification framework for skin cancer detection. The model classifies skin lesions into discrete categories: benign (benign skin moles/melanocytic lesions) and malignant (malignant skin cancer/melanoma). The ensemble classifier combines CNN, LSTM, and Bi-GRU models to predict these diagnostic labels. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Specificity, Sensitivity, F-measure, and MCC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (Dataset 1)
datasets_used.description: A balanced dataset from the ISIC Archive, an open-source public-access repository of skin images. It contains dermoscopic images of benign and malignant skin moles.

use.training: Used for training with varying learning percentages (60%, 70%, 80%, 90%) as detailed in Table 4.
use.testing: Used for testing with varying learning percentages (60%, 70%, 80%, 90%) as detailed in Table 4.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Dataset 2)
datasets_used.description: Also referred to as Skin cancer MNIST. This dataset consists of 10,015 dermatoscopic images collected from different populations using several modalities. It was used as a training set for scholarly machine learning applications.

use.training: Used for training with varying learning percentages (60%, 70%, 80%, 90%) as detailed in Table 4.
use.testing: Used for testing with varying learning percentages (60%, 70%, 80%, 90%) as detailed in Table 4.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compares performance metrics (accuracy, error, etc.) of the proposed model against others using descriptive statistics and tables/figures, noting that the proposed model yields 'better outcomes' and 'significantly lower' FPR, but without statistical significance testing.

EC + HUBM-GMR.notes: Outperformed EC + BMO, EC + HHOA, EC + HBA, EC + BES, CSO, GWO, EC + FF, AE, and plain + hierarchical classifier. Best results observed at Learning Percentage (LP) = 90.

EC + HUBM-GMR.mean_error: Lower than EC + HBA, EC + BMO, EC + HHO, EC + BES, GWO, EC + FF, and CSO
EC + HUBM-GMR.notes: Outperformed EC + BMO, EC + HHOA, EC + HBA, EC + BES, CSO, GWO, EC + FF, AE, and plain + hierarchical classifier. Best results observed at Learning Percentage (LP) = 90.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid optimization assisted deep ensemble classification framework combining Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Unit (Bi-GRU)

architecture_details.optimization_method: Hawks Updated Blue Monkey Algorithm with Gaussian Map Randomness (HUBM-GMR)
architecture_details.optimization_target: Weights of CNN, LSTM, and Bi-GRU models
feature_extraction: Improved CSLTP (Center Symmetric Local Ternary Pattern)

training_details.optimizer_algorithm: HUBM-GMR (Hybrid of Blue Monkey Optimization and Harris Hawks Optimization Algorithm)

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Averaging outputs of CNN, LSTM, and Bi-GRU
classification_or_output_head.output: Presence/Absence of Cancer (Binary Classification: Benign vs. Malignant)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Cat Swarm Optimization with Deep Learning (ASCDC-CSODL)
Hawks Updated Blue Monkey Algorithm with Gaussian Map Randomness (HUBM-GMR)
preprocessing_techniques: Median Filter (MF) for noise reduction and smoothing while maintaining edge information
Comparison with other filters: Mean filtering, Gaussian filtering, and CLAHE filtering
Removal of artifacts such as body hair and air bubbles via median filtering
Segmentation using Improved DBSCAN to isolate the Region of Interest (ROI)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as a technique to augment the set of 122 malignant and 196 benign skin lesion speckle visuals for deep learning, leveraging the statistical homogeneity of the patterns. (Note: This is cited from a related work [reference not fully specified in context, likely [36] or similar in literature review] rather than the primary proposed method).

The paper mentions that 'data augmentation' was employed in a cited ROI-based Transfer Learning strategy (Rehan et al. [24]) for DermIS and DermQuest datasets, but does not specify the specific geometric or color transforms used in that cited work. The primary proposed method in this paper does not explicitly list data augmentation techniques for its own training pipeline, relying instead on feature extraction (GLCM, LBP, LVP, CSLTP) and optimization (HUBM-GMR).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid optimization assisted deep ensemble classification framework (EC + HUBM-GMR) outperformed existing methods including EC + BMO, EC + HHOA, EC + HBA, EC + BES, CSO, GWO, EC + FF, AE, and plain + hierarchical classifiers in skin cancer detection.

The ensemble classifier combining CNN, LSTM, and Bi-GRU models, with weights tuned by the Hawks Updated Blue Monkey Algorithm with Gaussian Map Randomness (HUBM-GMR), achieved superior performance compared to other optimization techniques.

The framework achieved the best classification results at a Learning Percentage (LP) of 90, yielding an accuracy of approximately 0.94 (94%) and a significantly lower False Positive Rate (FPR) of 0.04.

The proposed method demonstrated a lower mean error (0.08) compared to comparative metaheuristic schemes (EC + HBA, EC + BMO, EC + HHO, EC + BES, GWO, EC + FF, and CSO) across both the ISIC and HAM10000 datasets.
The use of Improved DBSCAN for segmentation and specific texture features (GLCM, LBP, LVP, and improved CSLTP) contributed to the enhanced effectiveness of the skin cancer classification model.

The study addresses challenges in early skin cancer diagnosis by providing a non-contact automated diagnostic tool that mitigates the variability and limited accuracy (75-84%) of human dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant), excluding other clinically significant skin cancer types (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) present in the HAM10000 dataset.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison of classification metrics; it relies on descriptive statistics and claims superiority without rigorous statistical validation.

The dataset split methodology is unclear; the paper uses 'Learning Percentages' (60%, 70%, 80%, 90%) for training/testing but does not specify the validation set size or the specific train/validation/test split ratios, raising potential data leakage or overfitting concerns.

The HAM10000 dataset is inherently highly imbalanced across its seven classes, yet the paper reports using only 1,272 samples (presumably binary subset) without detailing how class imbalance was handled during training.

The proposed method relies on handcrafted texture features (GLCM, LBP, LVP, CSLTP) combined with deep learning, which may limit its generalizability compared to end-to-end deep learning models that learn feature representations automatically.

The optimization algorithm (HUBM-GMR) is a novel hybrid metaheuristic; its computational complexity and training time are not explicitly compared against standard optimizers, which is a concern for clinical deployment efficiency.

The study lacks external validation on independent, multi-center datasets; validation is restricted to two public datasets (ISIC and HAM10000), limiting the assessment of robustness to population or device bias.

The paper mentions 'patch cropping' for data augmentation in the literature review (citing other works) but does not clearly specify if this technique was applied to the primary proposed model's training pipeline, creating ambiguity about the extent of data augmentation used.
